englishDefinition: the way a person or group acts
example: Human behaviour often follows familiar patterns.
sourceSentence: But the behaviours that never change are history's most powerful lessons.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: to show or suggest what something will be like before it happens
example: Past choices can preview future risks.
sourceSentence: Because they preview what to expect in the future.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: to damage something badly and make it unable to work well
example: A power failure can cripple a city's transport system.
sourceSentence: A single event can cripple a carefully built system.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: connected with how geography influences politics and international relations
example: Energy supply can become a geopolitical issue.
sourceSentence: These needs shape markets, families, classrooms, and even geopolitical decisions.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: a form of a language used by a particular group or area
example: Online communities often create their own dialect.
sourceSentence: A new dialect of technology may appear.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

chineseDefinition: 永恆的；不受時間影響的
englishDefinition: not affected by changes in time or fashion
sourceSentence: We can derive wiser choices from timeless patterns.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: to think carefully about something for a long time
example: Students need time to contemplate complex questions.
sourceSentence: When we contemplate the intersection of fear and hope...
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: closely connected with the subject or situation
example: Choose examples that are relevant to the question.
sourceSentence: The human questions underneath remain relevant.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: to get something from a source or origin
example: We derive meaning from repeated experience.
sourceSentence: We can derive wiser choices from timeless patterns.
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World

englishDefinition: a point where two or more things meet
example: The lesson sits at the intersection of history and psychology.
sourceSentence: When we contemplate the intersection of fear and hope...
sourceTitle: What Never Changes in a Changing World
